---
title: "Methods — joint-4PL vs. classical two-stage bias simulation"
date: 2026-05-13
author: "Daniel Noble (with Claude assistance)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

Draft methods section for a controlled simulation that compares the joint
Bayesian 4PL estimator developed in this paper against the classical
two-stage TDT pipeline that is the field's current default. This note
documents exactly what has been done — DGP, estimators, sample sizes, and
the diagnostic checks — so the text can be moved into `ms/supplement.qmd`
after review. It deliberately does **not** make claims about which
estimator is preferable; the results and their interpretation are reported
elsewhere.

## Data-generating process

Each simulated dataset is one factorial experiment with five assay
temperatures ($T \in \{30, 32, 34, 36, 38\}\,^\circ\text{C}$) and six
exposure durations ($t \in \{1, 5, 15, 45, 135, 405\}$ minutes). Each
(temperature × duration) cell is replicated by $n_\text{reps}$ independent
cups. The per-cup sample size $N$ is drawn from a discrete uniform on
$\{10, 11, \dots, 20\}$, capturing the modest, unbalanced replication
typical of empirical TDT assays.

The latent survival probability follows a beta-binomial 4PL with
temperature acting only on the midpoint:

$$
p(T, t) \;=\; \ell \;+\; \frac{u - \ell}{1 + \exp\!\bigl[k\bigl(\log_{10} t - m(T)\bigr)\bigr]},
\qquad m(T) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,(T - \bar T).
$$

Truth parameters were fixed at $\ell = 0.05$, $u = 0.92$, $k = 8$,
$\beta_0 = 1.5$, $\beta_1 = -0.15$, $\bar T = 34\,^\circ\text{C}$, and
beta-binomial dispersion $\phi = 5$. The corresponding analytical targets,
derived in closed form from the DGP, are

$$
z_\text{true} \;=\; -\frac{1}{\beta_1} \;=\; 6.667\,^\circ\text{C},
\qquad
\text{CT}_{\max,\,1\text{h}}^{\text{true}} \;=\; \bar T \;+\; \frac{\log_{10} 60 - \beta_0 - k^{-1}\log\!\bigl[(u - 0.5)/(0.5 - \ell)\bigr]}{\beta_1}
\;=\; 32.094\,^\circ\text{C}.
$$

These are the per-simulation reference values against which both
estimators are scored. Survival counts are drawn beta-binomially: for each
cell, the cup-level probability $p^{(b)}$ is drawn from a
$\text{Beta}(p\phi, (1-p)\phi)$ distribution and the observed survivor count
from a $\text{Binomial}(N, p^{(b)})$. The data generator is in
[`scripts/sim_twostage_helpers.R`](../scripts/sim_twostage_helpers.R).

## Two design points

We ran the full simulation under two replication budgets, chosen to bracket
realistic small-to-moderate field designs:

| Design | $n_\text{reps}$ per cell | Total cups per dataset |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| n3     | 3                        | 90                     |
| n5     | 5                        | 150                    |

For each design we ran $N_\text{sim} = 1000$ datasets. The number of
simulations was set so that Monte Carlo standard errors (MCSEs) on the
performance summaries fell below pre-specified thresholds (see
[Sample size adequacy](#sec-mcse)).

## Estimators

Both estimators were applied to every simulated dataset, scored against the
same analytical truth, and summarised on the same basis (signed bias of the
point estimate, root-mean-square error, empirical 95% interval coverage,
and median 95% interval width).

### Joint Bayesian 4PL

The joint model is the estimator developed in this paper. It is a
non-linear `brms` formula with a `beta_binomial(link = "identity")` family
in which all four 4PL sub-parameters — the reparameterised lower asymptote,
upper asymptote, slope, and midpoint — carry a centred-temperature slope.
Default priors from `bayesTLS::make_4pl_priors()` were used (weakly
informative; specified in the supplement). Samples were drawn with
`cmdstanr` (2 chains × 2,000 iterations, half discarded as warm-up;
`adapt_delta = 0.9`, `max_treedepth = 12`). Post-fit, `z` and
$\text{CT}_{\max,\,1\text{h}}$ were extracted with
`bayesTLS::extract_tdt(..., lethal = TRUE)` using the standard
rate-multiplier definition of $T_\text{crit}$; we report the posterior
median and 2.5% / 97.5% quantiles.

### Classical two-stage

The two-stage pipeline implements the field-standard recipe. Stage 1: for
each temperature independently, a binomial GLM with logit link is fitted to
the survivor counts as a function of $\log_{10} t$; the per-temperature
$\log_{10} \text{LT}_{50}$ is read off as $-\hat\beta_0/\hat\beta_1$ with
standard error supplied by the delta method from the Stage-1 covariance.
Stage 2: an unweighted ordinary least-squares regression of those
$\log_{10}\text{LT}_{50}$ values on temperature gives slope and intercept
estimates from which $z = -1/\hat\beta_1^{(2)}$ and
$\text{CT}_{\max,\,1\text{h}} = (\log_{10} 60 - \hat\beta_0^{(2)})/\hat\beta_1^{(2)}$
follow. The 95% confidence interval is obtained by the delta method
applied to the Stage-2 covariance. A simulated dataset is marked as
two-stage success only when every temperature delivered a finite,
negative-slope Stage-1 fit and the Stage-2 slope itself was finite and
negative.

Unweighted OLS at Stage 2 was retained deliberately, matching the version
of the pipeline most commonly reported in the empirical TDT literature.
SE-weighted alternatives were not assessed in this run.

## Sample size adequacy {#sec-mcse}

Each performance summary $\widehat{Q}$ was reported with a Monte Carlo
standard error: $\text{MCSE}(\widehat{\text{bias}}) = s_b / \sqrt{N_\text{sim}}$
for mean bias, $\sqrt{p(1-p)/N_\text{sim}}$ for coverage, and the standard
delta-method form for RMSE. We pre-specified MCSE thresholds of 0.05 °C
for bias and 0.015 for coverage. At $N_\text{sim} = 1000$ both designs met
the thresholds for every method × quantity combination, so we did not
extend the simulation.

## Diagnostic and reproducibility checks

Each joint-4PL fit logs maximum $\hat R$ and divergent-transition count
post-warm-up. Across all 2,000 fits (n3 + n5) no fit exceeded the standard
$\hat R < 1.05$ threshold, and ≥ 95 % of fits returned zero divergences.

The full set of per-simulation results, including all $1{,}000$
posterior draws for $z$, $\text{CT}_{\max,\,1\text{h}}$ and
$T_\text{crit}$ per simulation, is stored as resume-safe per-sim RDS
files under `output/sim_twostage/raw/<design>/` and rolled up into
`summary_<design>.rds`, `meta_<design>.rds`, and `diffs_<design>.rds` at
the end of each run. The driver is
[`scripts/sim_twostage_bias.R`](../scripts/sim_twostage_bias.R); the
data-generating helpers and the two estimator wrappers live in
[`scripts/sim_twostage_helpers.R`](../scripts/sim_twostage_helpers.R).
Parallelism uses a PSOCK cluster (`parallel::makeCluster` + `parLapply`)
with five workers; a PSOCK cluster (rather than `mclapply`) is required
because `cmdstanr` launches Stan executables via `processx`, which is not
fork-safe under macOS.

## Open scope

This first simulation block holds the data-generating process fixed in two
respects: (i) temperature acts only on the 4PL midpoint, leaving the
asymptotes ($\ell$, $u$) and slope ($k$) constant in $T$; and (ii) the
joint-4PL estimator uses the package's default priors, with no sensitivity
analysis. Both restrictions are deliberate first-pass simplifications.
Extensions that vary the DGP (temperature acting on $\ell$, $u$, or $k$,
with or without midpoint preservation) and that vary the joint-4PL priors
are the natural next steps.
